title: Your Path: C++, raylib & the Native Toolchain
html:
Why C++ and raylib

You chose the hardest, most rewarding path: build every genre from scratch in C++ with raylib, a small C library that gives you a window, input, audio, textures, and 2D/3D drawing primitives — and nothing else. There is no editor, no scene graph, no physics, no entity system handed to you. By the end of this track you will have hand-coded a raycasting FPS, a pseudo-3D racer, a platformer, an RPG, a fighting game, and more. You will know C++ and game architecture inside-out because you wrote every line.

C++ gives you manual control over memory and zero-cost abstractions; raylib gives you a clean, beginner-friendly C API so you spend your energy on game logic instead of OpenGL boilerplate. The combination is how a huge number of indie engines actually start.

The native toolchain

Unlike scripting languages, C++ must be compiled to a native binary. Three tools make that happen:

  • A compiler — turns .cpp source into machine code. Use clang/Xcode CLT on macOS, MSVC (Visual Studio) on Windows, or g++ on Linux.
  • CMake — a cross-platform build generator. You describe your project once; CMake produces the right build files for any OS.
  • raylib — the library you link against.
Tip.
We use CMake's FetchContent to download and build raylib automatically. That means anyone who clones your repo just runs CMake — no manual library install, no version drift.

Install the compiler

shell
# macOS: command-line tools (clang, make, git) xcode-select --install # Ubuntu/Debian sudo apt install build-essential cmake git # Windows: install Visual Studio 2022 (Desktop C++) + CMake # or: winget install Kitware.CMake Git.Git

A clean project layout you will reuse

Every game in this track uses the same skeleton. Create it once and copy it forward:

layout
pong/ ├── CMakeLists.txt # build description ├── src/ │ └── main.cpp # your code └── assets/ # textures, sounds, fonts

CMakeLists.txt

CMakeLists.txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.16) project(game CXX) set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20) set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON) # Download + build raylib automatically include(FetchContent) FetchContent_Declare( raylib GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/raysan5/raylib.git GIT_TAG 5.5 ) set(BUILD_EXAMPLES OFF CACHE BOOL "" FORCE) FetchContent_MakeAvailable(raylib) add_executable(game src/main.cpp) target_link_libraries(game PRIVATE raylib)

main.cpp — your first window

src/main.cpp
#include "raylib.h" int main() { InitWindow(800, 450, "C++ + raylib"); SetTargetFPS(60); // The game loop: runs until the window is closed while (!WindowShouldClose()) { BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(RAYWHITE); DrawText("It compiles. You're a C++ game dev now.", 180, 200, 20, DARKGRAY); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

Build and run

From the project root, configure once then build whenever you change code. The first build is slow because it compiles raylib; later builds are fast.

shell
# Generate build files into a /build folder cmake -B build # Compile cmake --build build # Run (path differs per platform/generator) ./build/game # macOS / Linux # build\Debug\game.exe on Windows/MSVC
Mental model.
A C++ game is just a normal program with a main() that never returns until you close the window. InitWindow opens the OS window and an OpenGL context; the while loop is your game's heartbeat; CloseWindow frees everything.

What each raylib call does

CallPurpose
InitWindow(w,h,title)Create window + GPU context
WindowShouldClose()True when user hits X or Esc
BeginDrawing/EndDrawingOpen/flush a frame to the screen
ClearBackground(col)Wipe the frame to one color
SetTargetFPS(n)Cap the loop to n frames/sec
Gotcha.
Drawing only works between BeginDrawing() and EndDrawing(). Calling a Draw* function outside that pair silently does nothing. And never put your loop logic after the loop — that code runs only once the window is already closing.
🎮 Build This: Setup — Your Reusable Skeleton

Genre: Setup / toolchain. This is a concept lesson — it does not extend a prior game; it creates the project template that every game in this track will start from.

  1. Create the pong/ folder with src/ and assets/.
  2. Paste in the CMakeLists.txt and main.cpp above.
  3. Run cmake -B build then cmake --build build and launch the binary.
  4. Change the window title and the text, rebuild, and confirm your changes appear.
  5. Copy this folder to _template/ — you will duplicate it for Breakout, Snake, and beyond.

title: A Robust Game Loop, Fixed Timestep & Delta-Time
html:
The naive loop is a trap

The simplest loop moves things by a constant amount each frame: x += 5;. On a 60 Hz monitor that's 300 px/sec; on a 144 Hz monitor it's 720 px/sec. Your game runs faster on better hardware — a classic bug that broke countless old PC games. We fix this with two complementary techniques: delta-time and a fixed-timestep accumulator.

Delta-time: scale by elapsed seconds

Instead of moving per frame, move per second. raylib gives you the seconds since the last frame via GetFrameTime(). Multiply your speed (in units/second) by it:

delta.cpp
float x = 100.0f; const float speed = 300.0f; // pixels per SECOND while (!WindowShouldClose()) { float dt = GetFrameTime(); // e.g. 0.0166s at 60fps x += speed * dt; // same 300px/sec everywhere // ...draw... }
Rule.
Any value that changes over time — position, timers, animation — must be multiplied by dt. If you ever write a raw += 5 in update code, stop and add * dt.

Why delta-time alone isn't enough

Delta-time keeps speed consistent, but variable dt makes physics and collision nondeterministic. A huge frame spike (alt-tab, a hitch) produces a giant dt, and an object can tunnel straight through a wall in one step. Networked and replay-based games also need the simulation to step in identical, reproducible chunks. The answer is the fixed timestep: simulate the world in constant slices (say 1/60 s) regardless of render rate.

The fixed-timestep accumulator

We keep a time bank (the accumulator). Each frame we add the real elapsed time to it, then run as many fixed-size updates as fit. Leftover time stays in the bank for next frame.

fixed_loop.cpp
#include "raylib.h" const double STEP = 1.0 / 60.0; // fixed sim step (seconds) struct Ball { float x, prevX, vx; }; int main() { InitWindow(800, 450, "Fixed timestep"); SetTargetFPS(0); // uncapped: prove it's framerate-independent Ball b{100, 100, 200}; // vx in px/sec double acc = 0.0; while (!WindowShouldClose()) { acc += GetFrameTime(); // Run zero or more FIXED updates while (acc >= STEP) { b.prevX = b.x; // save for interpolation b.x += b.vx * (float)STEP; if (b.x > 760 || b.x < 40) b.vx = -b.vx; acc -= STEP; } // How far between sim steps are we? 0..1 float alpha = (float)(acc / STEP); float drawX = b.prevX + (b.x - b.prevX) * alpha; BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(BLACK); DrawCircle((int)drawX, 225, 20, RAYWHITE); DrawFPS(10, 10); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); }

Frame interpolation: smooth at any FPS

When the render rate is higher than 60 Hz, multiple frames draw between two sim steps. If you draw the raw b.x, motion stutters. Instead we interpolate between the previous and current state by alpha (the fraction of a step still sitting in the accumulator). That's why we stored prevX. The result: buttery-smooth motion that's also perfectly deterministic.

The pattern.
Accumulate real time → step the sim in fixed slices → interpolate the render between the last two states. This single structure underlies the platformer, the physics puzzle, and the fighting game later in this track.

Two safety details

  • Spiral of death: if a single frame is catastrophically slow, the accumulator can demand more steps than you can run, making things worse. Clamp it: if (frame > 0.25) frame = 0.25; before adding.
  • Separate update from render: never call Draw* inside the fixed-update while. Update mutates state; render reads it once per frame.
TermMeaning
dtReal seconds since last frame
STEPConstant sim slice (1/60 s)
accumulatorUnspent time bank
alphaRender blend factor 0..1
Gotcha.
Use double for the accumulator and step math. A 32-bit float loses precision after the game has run for a while, causing tiny timing drift you'll never track down.
🎮 Build This: Engine Core — The Timing Heartbeat

Genre: Engine core. Concept lesson — it does not extend a prior game. You're building the timing skeleton that the upcoming Pong (and every later game) drops its logic into.

  1. Start from your Lesson 1 template and paste in the fixed-timestep loop.
  2. Set SetTargetFPS(0) and watch the ball move identically at 60, 144, and uncapped FPS.
  3. Add a clamp so GetFrameTime() never exceeds 0.25 s; alt-tab and confirm no teleport.
  4. Toggle interpolation off (draw b.x directly) and notice the stutter, then turn it back on.
  5. Add a second ball at a different speed to confirm both stay framerate-independent.

title: Input Handling & Your First Game: Pong
html:
Reading input cleanly

raylib polls device state every frame; you query it. There are two flavors of query and the difference matters constantly:

  • Held — IsKeyDown(KEY_W) is true every frame the key is pressed. Use for continuous movement.
  • Edge — IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE) is true only on the single frame it goes down. Use for jumps, shooting, menu confirms.
input.cpp
// Keyboard if (IsKeyDown(KEY_W)) p1y -= speed * dt; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)) Serve(); // Mouse Vector2 m = GetMousePosition(); if (IsMouseButtonPressed(MOUSE_LEFT_BUTTON)) {} // Gamepad (player 0, left analog stick Y) if (IsGamepadAvailable(0)) { float ly = GetGamepadAxisMovement(0, GAMEPAD_AXIS_LEFT_Y); if (fabsf(ly) > 0.2f) p1y += ly * speed * dt; // deadzone }
Deadzone.
Analog sticks never rest at exactly 0. Ignore tiny magnitudes (fabsf(axis) > 0.2f) or your paddle drifts on its own.

Pong: the data

Pong is the perfect first game: two paddles, one ball, a score. We model state as plain structs — no classes needed yet. This is the first game of our classic arcade genre block.

pong.cpp (state)
const int W = 800, H = 450; struct Paddle { float x, y, w = 14, h = 90; int score = 0; }; struct Ball { float x, y, vx, vy, r = 8; }; Paddle left { 30, H/2 - 45 }; Paddle right { W - 44, H/2 - 45 }; Ball ball { W/2, H/2, 260, 160 };

The update step

Move paddles from input, clamp them to the screen, move the ball, and bounce it off the top and bottom walls. We feed all of this through dt so it obeys Lesson 2's framerate-independence.

pong.cpp (update)
void UpdatePaddle(Paddle& p, int up, int down, float dt) { const float spd = 380.0f; if (IsKeyDown(up)) p.y -= spd * dt; if (IsKeyDown(down)) p.y += spd * dt; if (p.y < 0) p.y = 0; if (p.y + p.h > H) p.y = H - p.h; } void UpdateBall(Ball& b, float dt) { b.x += b.vx * dt; b.y += b.vy * dt; if (b.y - b.r < 0 || b.y + b.r > H) b.vy = -b.vy; }

Paddle collision and scoring

A ball-vs-paddle test is a circle against a rectangle. raylib ships CheckCollisionCircleRec, which does exactly that. On a hit we flip vx and nudge the vertical speed based on where the ball struck the paddle — hitting near an edge sends it off at a sharper angle, which is the soul of Pong's feel.

pong.cpp (collision + score)
void Bounce(Ball& b, const Paddle& p, int dir) { Rectangle r { p.x, p.y, p.w, p.h }; if (CheckCollisionCircleRec({b.x, b.y}, b.r, r)) { b.vx = dir * fabsf(b.vx) * 1.05f; // speed up + push out float rel = (b.y - (p.y + p.h/2)) / (p.h/2); b.vy = rel * 300.0f; // angle by hit position } } void Score(Ball& b, Paddle& l, Paddle& r) { if (b.x < 0) { r.score++; b = { W/2, H/2, 260, 160 }; } if (b.x > W) { l.score++; b = { W/2, H/2, -260, 160 }; } }

Drawing it

pong.cpp (render)
BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(BLACK); DrawRectangle(left.x, left.y, left.w, left.h, RAYWHITE); DrawRectangle(right.x, right.y, right.w, right.h, RAYWHITE); DrawCircle(ball.x, ball.y, ball.r, RAYWHITE); DrawText(TextFormat("%d %d", left.score, right.score), W/2 - 40, 20, 40, GRAY); EndDrawing();
Architecture seed.
Notice the shape: read input → update state → resolve collisions → draw. Every game in this track is a variation on those four phases. Pong just has the fewest moving parts.
Gotcha.
Multiplying vx by 1.05 each hit makes the ball accelerate forever. Cap it: if (fabsf(b.vx) > 600) b.vx = copysignf(600, b.vx); so rallies stay playable.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Pong

Genre: classic arcade. This starts a new genre — your first complete game. It is the base that the next lesson (Breakout) will extend.

  1. Copy the template; add the Paddle and Ball structs and the three update functions.
  2. Wire Player 1 to W/S and Player 2 to Up/Down; add gamepad axis as an alternate for P1.
  3. Call Bounce for each paddle and Score each frame; render paddles, ball, and score.
  4. Cap the ball speed; add a SPACE serve so a point pauses until pressed.
  5. First to 11 wins — draw a win screen and let R reset both scores.

title: Collision Detection & Breakout
html:
The two collision shapes you'll use forever

Pong already used a circle-vs-rectangle test. Breakout demands you understand collision properly: detecting overlap, knowing which side was hit, and resolving it so objects don't stick. The two workhorse shapes in 2D are the AABB (axis-aligned bounding box) and the circle.

AABB vs AABB

Two boxes overlap only if they overlap on both axes. This is the single most useful collision test in all of 2D gaming:

aabb.cpp
struct Rect { float x, y, w, h; }; bool Overlap(const Rect& a, const Rect& b) { return a.x < b.x + b.w && a.x + a.w > b.x && a.y < b.y + b.h && a.y + a.h > b.y; }

Circle vs AABB (the ball)

Find the closest point on the box to the circle's center by clamping, then check if that point is within the radius. This tells us not just if but where they touch:

circle_rect.cpp
float Clamp(float v, float lo, float hi) { return v < lo ? lo : (v > hi ? hi : v); } bool CircleRect(float cx, float cy, float r, const Rect& b, float& nx, float& ny) { float px = Clamp(cx, b.x, b.x + b.w); float py = Clamp(cy, b.y, b.y + b.h); float dx = cx - px, dy = cy - py; if (dx*dx + dy*dy > r*r) return false; nx = dx; ny = dy; // vector from box to ball return true; }

Reflection vs resolution

Two distinct jobs. Reflection changes velocity direction; resolution pushes the overlapping object back out so it doesn't register the same hit twice. The (nx, ny) vector tells us which axis dominated the collision — that's the axis we flip.

bounce.cpp
// nx,ny from CircleRect. Bounce on the dominant axis. if (CircleRect(ball.x, ball.y, ball.r, brick.box, nx, ny)) { if (fabsf(nx) > fabsf(ny)) ball.vx = -ball.vx; // side hit else ball.vy = -ball.vy; // top/bottom brick.alive = false; }
Sticky-ball gotcha.
If you only flip velocity but leave the ball overlapping, next frame it's still inside and flips again — it jitters or sticks. Always nudge it out: ball.x += nx; ball.y += ny; after reflecting, or move the ball before testing and undo the move on a hit.

Breakout: the brick grid

Breakout extends Pong: same paddle, same bouncing ball, but the second paddle becomes a wall of destructible bricks. We lay them out as a 2D grid.

bricks.cpp
const int COLS = 10, ROWS = 6; struct Brick { Rect box; bool alive = true; Color col; }; Brick bricks[ROWS][COLS]; void InitBricks() { float bw = 72, bh = 24, pad = 6, top = 50; for (int r = 0; r < ROWS; r++) for (int c = 0; c < COLS; c++) { bricks[r][c].box = { 10 + c*(bw+pad), top + r*(bh+pad), bw, bh }; bricks[r][c].alive = true; bricks[r][c].col = ColorFromHSV(r * 40.0f, 0.7f, 0.9f); } }

Update loop: ball, walls, paddle, bricks

breakout_update.cpp
ball.x += ball.vx * dt; ball.y += ball.vy * dt; if (ball.x < ball.r || ball.x > W - ball.r) ball.vx = -ball.vx; if (ball.y < ball.r) ball.vy = -ball.vy; if (ball.y > H) LoseLife(); // fell off bottom float nx, ny; if (CircleRect(ball.x, ball.y, ball.r, paddle, nx, ny)) { ball.vy = -fabsf(ball.vy); float rel = (ball.x - (paddle.x + paddle.w/2))/(paddle.w/2); ball.vx = rel * 300.0f; // steer by hit spot } for (auto& row : bricks) for (auto& brk : row) { if (!brk.alive) continue; if (CircleRect(ball.x, ball.y, ball.r, brk.box, nx, ny)) { (fabsf(nx) > fabsf(ny)) ? ball.vx = -ball.vx : ball.vy = -ball.vy; brk.alive = false; score += 10; break; } }
Why break.
Resolve only one brick per frame. Handling several simultaneously double-flips the velocity and the ball behaves chaotically. One hit, then re-test next frame.

Power-ups

When a brick dies, occasionally drop a falling capsule. If the paddle catches it, apply an effect — widen the paddle, multiply the ball, or grant a sticky catch. Model it as a small falling AABB tested against the paddle with Overlap.

Power-upEffect
Widepaddle.w *= 1.5f for 10 s
MultiSpawn 2 extra balls
SlowScale all ball velocities 0.7×
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Breakout

Genre: classic arcade. This extends Pong — keep the paddle and bouncing ball; replace the opponent with a destructible brick grid and a bottom-out lose condition.

  1. Reuse Pong's paddle (now bottom-mounted, moving left/right) and ball.
  2. Add the CircleRect helper and the ROWS×COLS brick grid via InitBricks.
  3. Resolve ball vs walls, paddle, and bricks; break after the first brick hit.
  4. Track score and 3 lives; lose a life when the ball passes the bottom.
  5. On brick death, 1-in-6 chance to drop a power-up capsule; catch with the paddle.
  6. Win when all bricks are cleared, then load the next layout.

title: Vector Math, Grids & Snake
html:
2D vectors are the language of games

A vector is just an (x, y) pair, but it represents direction and magnitude at once. Position, velocity, the direction to an enemy — all vectors. raylib defines Vector2 and raymath.h gives you operations, but to truly own C++ you should be able to write them yourself:

vec2.cpp
struct Vec2 { float x = 0, y = 0; Vec2 operator+(Vec2 o) const { return {x+o.x, y+o.y}; } Vec2 operator-(Vec2 o) const { return {x-o.x, y-o.y}; } Vec2 operator*(float s) const { return {x*s, y*s}; } }; float Dot(Vec2 a, Vec2 b) { return a.x*b.x + a.y*b.y; } float Length(Vec2 v) { return sqrtf(Dot(v, v)); } Vec2 Normalize(Vec2 v) { float l = Length(v); return l > 0 ? Vec2{v.x/l, v.y/l} : Vec2{0,0}; }

The three operations to internalize

OpReturnsUse for
Length(v)MagnitudeDistance, speed checks
Normalize(v)Unit vectorPure direction, then × speed
Dot(a,b)ScalarAngle/alignment: >0 same way, 0 perpendicular, <0 opposite
Distance trick.
Comparing distances? Skip the sqrtf — compare squared lengths. Dot(d,d) < r*r is the same test as Length(d) < r but faster, since square root is expensive.

Grids: the discrete world

Snake doesn't move freely — it lives on a grid. The whole board is conceptually a 2D array of cells. Convert between pixel space and grid space by dividing or multiplying by the cell size:

grid.cpp
const int CELL = 24; const int COLS = 25, ROWS = 18; // 600 x 432 board // grid cell -> pixel top-left int px = gridX * CELL; int py = gridY * CELL; // pixel -> grid cell int gx = mouseX / CELL; int gy = mouseY / CELL;

Snake: state and movement

The snake is a list of integer grid cells; the head is element 0. A direction vector (also integer, one of the four cardinals) is added to the head each tick. We use std::deque because we push a new head and pop the tail every step. This is a new genre: grid-based arcade.

snake.cpp (state)
#include <deque> struct Cell { int x, y; }; std::deque<Cell> snake { {12,9}, {11,9}, {10,9} }; Cell dir { 1, 0 }; // moving right Cell food { 18, 9 }; bool grew = false; double tickTimer = 0; const double TICK = 0.12;

Discrete time: the tick

Snake updates on a fixed tick, not every frame — otherwise it'd zip across the board. Accumulate dt (Lesson 2!) and step the snake only when a tick elapses. Input is read every frame but only the latest queued direction applies at the next tick.

snake.cpp (update)
// Read input every frame; block 180° reversals if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_UP) && dir.y == 0) dir = {0,-1}; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_DOWN) && dir.y == 0) dir = {0, 1}; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_LEFT) && dir.x == 0) dir = {-1,0}; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_RIGHT) && dir.x == 0) dir = {1, 0}; tickTimer += GetFrameTime(); if (tickTimer >= TICK) { tickTimer -= TICK; Cell head = snake.front(); Cell nh { head.x + dir.x, head.y + dir.y }; // Wrap around the edges nh.x = (nh.x + COLS) % COLS; nh.y = (nh.y + ROWS) % ROWS; // Self-collision = game over for (auto& c : snake) if (c.x == nh.x && c.y == nh.y) GameOver(); snake.push_front(nh); if (nh.x == food.x && nh.y == food.y) { score++; SpawnFood(); // don't pop -> snake grows } else { snake.pop_back(); // normal move } }
Elegant insight.
Growth is the absence of a tail pop. Every tick you add a head; normally you also drop the tail so length stays constant. Eat food → skip the drop → the snake is one cell longer. No separate grow logic needed.

Spawning food without overlap

snake.cpp (food)
void SpawnFood() { while (true) { Cell f { GetRandomValue(0, COLS-1), GetRandomValue(0, ROWS-1) }; bool onSnake = false; for (auto& c : snake) if (c.x==f.x && c.y==f.y) onSnake = true; if (!onSnake) { food = f; return; } } }
Gotcha.
Reversing direction (e.g. pressing Left while moving Right) walks the head straight into the neck and instantly ends the game. The dir.x == 0 / dir.y == 0 guards above prevent illegal 180° turns.
🎮 Build This: Grid Arcade — Snake

Genre: grid-based arcade. This starts a new genre — discrete grid movement instead of Pong/Breakout's free pixel motion.

  1. Build the Vec2 helpers and confirm Normalize/Dot/Length with quick prints.
  2. Set up the CELL/COLS/ROWS grid and a deque snake of 3 cells.
  3. Drive movement on a fixed TICK using a dt accumulator; block reversals.
  4. Implement edge wrapping with modulo and self-collision game-over.
  5. Add food: grow by skipping the tail pop; respawn food off the snake body.
  6. Render cells as filled rects; speed up the tick slightly every few foods eaten.

title: Matrices, Rotation & Asteroids
html:
Rotation, transforms, and why matrices matter

Asteroids needs a ship that points in any direction, thrusts the way it's facing, and drifts with inertia. That requires rotation. Under the hood, rotating a point by angle θ is a 2×2 matrix multiply, but the practical formula you'll use constantly is:

rotate.cpp
// Rotate (x,y) by angle a (radians) around origin Vec2 Rotate(Vec2 p, float a) { float c = cosf(a), s = sinf(a); return { p.x*c - p.y*s, // = | cos -sin | | x | p.x*s + p.y*c }; // | sin cos | | y | } // Unit "forward" vector for a heading angle Vec2 Forward(float a) { return { cosf(a), sinf(a) }; }

That 2×2 grid is the rotation matrix. A full transform (translate + rotate + scale) is a 3×3 matrix; for a 2D arcade game the explicit formula above is clearer and faster. raylib also offers DrawPolyLinesEx and lets you pass a rotation directly when drawing, but understanding the math lets you transform your own ship vertices.

Radians vs degrees.
C++'s sinf/cosf take radians. raylib's draw rotations take degrees. Convert with deg = rad * RAD2DEG (both constants ship in raylib). Mixing them up is the number-one rotation bug.

The velocity integrator

Asteroids' signature feel is inertia: thrust adds to velocity, and you keep drifting after you let go. That's a simple integrator — exactly the semi-implicit Euler step we formalize later for the platformer:

ship.cpp
struct Ship { Vec2 pos { 400, 225 }; Vec2 vel { 0, 0 }; float angle = -PI/2; // pointing up }; void UpdateShip(Ship& s, float dt) { const float turn = 4.0f, thrust = 280.0f, drag = 0.6f; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_LEFT)) s.angle -= turn * dt; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) s.angle += turn * dt; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_UP)) s.vel = s.vel + Forward(s.angle) * (thrust * dt); s.vel = s.vel * (1.0f - drag * dt); // space friction (arcadey) s.pos = s.pos + s.vel * dt; // integrate position }

Screen wrapping

Fly off one edge, reappear on the opposite side. Wrap every wrappable entity through one helper using modulo-style math:

wrap.cpp
void Wrap(Vec2& p, float w, float h) { if (p.x < 0) p.x += w; if (p.x >= w) p.x -= w; if (p.y < 0) p.y += h; if (p.y >= h) p.y -= h; }

Drawing the ship as transformed geometry

Define the ship in local space (centered on origin, pointing along +X), then rotate and translate each vertex into world space. This local-to-world transform is the heart of every rotated sprite and 3D mesh you'll ever render.

draw_ship.cpp
void DrawShip(const Ship& s) { Vec2 local[3] = { {16,0}, {-12,-10}, {-12,10} }; Vec2 w[3]; for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) w[i] = Rotate(local[i], s.angle) + s.pos; DrawTriangleLines({w[0].x,w[0].y}, {w[1].x,w[1].y}, {w[2].x,w[2].y}, RAYWHITE); }

Asteroids that split

Each rock has a size tier. Shooting a big one removes it and spawns two smaller rocks moving in random directions; smallest tier just dies. Collision is circle-vs-circle using our squared-length trick from Lesson 5.

rock.cpp
struct Rock { Vec2 pos, vel; float r; int tier; }; std::vector<Rock> rocks; void SplitRock(int i, std::vector<Rock>& out) { Rock hit = rocks[i]; rocks.erase(rocks.begin() + i); if (hit.tier == 1) return; // smallest: gone for (int k = 0; k < 2; k++) { float a = GetRandomValue(0, 360) * DEG2RAD; out.push_back({ hit.pos, Forward(a) * 90.0f, hit.r * 0.5f, hit.tier - 1 }); } } bool Hit(Vec2 a, Vec2 b, float r) { Vec2 d = a - b; return Dot(d, d) < r*r; // squared dist }
Iteration gotcha.
Never erase from a vector while a range-for is iterating it — you invalidate the loop. Collect splits in a separate out vector (as above), then append after the loop, or iterate backwards by index.
Pattern carried forward.
Local-space geometry + a rotation transform + a velocity integrator is the exact toolkit you'll reuse for the raycasting FPS player and the pseudo-3D racer's car. Master it here in 2D.
🎮 Build This: Vector Arcade — Asteroids

Genre: vector arcade. This starts a new genre — free rotation and momentum, unlike Snake's grid or Breakout's axis-bouncing.

  1. Build the Rotate/Forward helpers and the Ship with thrust, turn, and drag.
  2. Integrate velocity each frame and Wrap the ship around the screen.
  3. Fire bullets on SPACE as small entities with Forward(angle) * speed velocity; wrap and expire them.
  4. Spawn several tier-3 rocks with random velocities; wrap them too.
  5. On bullet-vs-rock hit (squared-distance test), call SplitRock; add score per tier.
  6. End the game if a rock hits the ship; respawn with brief invulnerability.

title: Sprites, Spritesheet Animation & Space Invaders
html:
From shapes to sprites

So far we've drawn rectangles, circles, and lines. Real games use textures — images loaded onto the GPU. raylib loads an image file into a Texture2D with one call, and draws all or part of it with DrawTexturePro.

texture.cpp
Texture2D tex = LoadTexture("assets/invaders.png"); // ...use it... UnloadTexture(tex); // free GPU memory when done
Load once, not every frame.
LoadTexture uploads to the GPU and is expensive. Load before the loop, draw inside it, unload after. Loading inside the loop will tank your framerate and leak memory. (Lesson 8 wraps this in RAII so you can't forget.)

Spritesheets: many frames, one image

A spritesheet packs many frames into a single texture (fewer GPU uploads, fewer draw-call switches). You draw a sub-rectangle — the source rect — onto a destination rect on screen. To animate, you slide the source rect across the sheet over time.

spritesheet.cpp
// A sheet of FRAMES horizontal cells, each FW x FH pixels. const int FRAMES = 2, FW = 32, FH = 32; struct Anim { int frame = 0; double t = 0; double rate = 0.5; }; void StepAnim(Anim& a, double dt) { a.t += dt; if (a.t >= a.rate) { a.t -= a.rate; a.frame = (a.frame+1) % FRAMES; } } void DrawSprite(Texture2D tex, int frame, Vec2 pos) { Rectangle src { (float)frame*FW, 0, FW, FH }; Rectangle dst { pos.x, pos.y, FW*2.0f, FH*2.0f }; // 2x scale DrawTexturePro(tex, src, dst, {0,0}, 0, WHITE); }
Source rect = your scalpel.
DrawTexturePro(tex, source, dest, origin, rotation, tint) draws any rectangle of the texture, scaled, rotated, and tinted, into any rectangle on screen. It's the one draw call you'll use for almost everything sprite-related.

Space Invaders: marching aliens

This starts a new genre: the shoot-em-up. The aliens move as a single block: all step right until the rightmost touches the edge, then the whole grid drops down a row and reverses. We track a shared direction and speed for the formation.

invaders.cpp (state)
const int AC = 11, AR = 5; struct Alien { Vec2 pos; bool alive = true; }; Alien aliens[AR][AC]; int marchDir = 1; // +1 right, -1 left float marchSpeed = 30.0f; // px/sec, ramps up as they die Anim marchAnim;

The formation step

invaders.cpp (march)
void March(float dt) { float dx = marchDir * marchSpeed * dt; bool edge = false; for (auto& row : aliens) for (auto& a : row) { if (!a.alive) continue; a.pos.x += dx; if (a.pos.x < 10 || a.pos.x > 800-10-FW*2) edge = true; } if (edge) { // reverse + drop the whole block marchDir = -marchDir; for (auto& row : aliens) for (auto& a : row) a.pos.y += 18; } }
Classic feel.
Speed scales with how few aliens remain — recompute marchSpeed from the alive count each frame. The last lone invader skittering at top speed is the iconic tension of Space Invaders, and it's free: fewer aliens → fewer pixels moved → recompute → faster.

Shooting and resolution

The player fires one bullet at a time upward; aliens occasionally drop bombs. Bullet-vs-alien is an AABB test (Lesson 4). On a hit, kill the alien, free the bullet, add score.

invaders.cpp (shooting)
struct Bullet { Vec2 pos; bool live = false; }; Bullet shot; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE) && !shot.live) { shot.live = true; shot.pos = { player.x+14, player.y }; } if (shot.live) { shot.pos.y -= 520 * dt; if (shot.pos.y < 0) shot.live = false; for (auto& row : aliens) for (auto& a : row) { if (!a.alive) continue; Rectangle ab { a.pos.x, a.pos.y, FW*2, FH*2 }; if (shot.live && CheckCollisionPointRec({shot.pos.x, shot.pos.y}, ab)) { a.alive = false; shot.live = false; score += 10; } } }

Waves

When every alien is dead, reset the grid, lower the start row slightly, and bump base speed: that's the next wave. When aliens reach the player's row, it's game over.

EventEffect
All aliens deadNew wave, faster, lower start
Alien reaches bottomGame over
Bomb hits playerLose a life
🎮 Build This: Shoot-em-up — Space Invaders

Genre: shoot-em-up. This starts a new genre. It reuses AABB collision (Breakout) and the dt loop, but introduces textures and spritesheet animation.

  1. Load a 2-frame alien spritesheet; build the StepAnim/DrawSprite helpers.
  2. Lay out an 11×5 alien grid; implement March with edge-reverse-and-drop.
  3. Scale marchSpeed by the count of alive aliens each frame.
  4. Add a single player bullet on SPACE; resolve bullet-vs-alien with AABB.
  5. Have aliens randomly drop bombs; AABB them against the player for lives.
  6. Clear the grid → spawn a faster wave; aliens reaching the bottom = game over.

title: Clean C++ Architecture: Entities, Components, RAII & a Scene Stack
html:
Why refactor now

You've shipped five games with plain structs and global state. That's exactly the right way to learn, but it doesn't scale: the platformer, the RPG, and the FPS each need menus, pause screens, many entity types, and resources that must be freed reliably. This lesson builds the three reusable architecture pillars every later game in this track depends on: RAII resource wrappers, a lightweight entity/component model, and a push/pop scene stack.

RAII: resources that free themselves

In Space Invaders you had to remember UnloadTexture. Forget it and you leak GPU memory. RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) ties a resource's lifetime to an object: acquire in the constructor, release in the destructor. When the object goes out of scope, C++ guarantees the destructor runs — even on early return or exception.

texture_raii.hpp
class Tex { Texture2D t_{}; public: explicit Tex(const char* path) { t_ = LoadTexture(path); } ~Tex() { UnloadTexture(t_); } // auto-freed // No copying (would double-free); allow moving. Tex(const Tex&) = delete; Tex& operator=(const Tex&) = delete; Tex(Tex&& o) noexcept { t_ = o.t_; o.t_.id = 0; } const Texture2D& get() const { return t_; } };
The C++ superpower.
Deleting the copy constructor and writing a move constructor is the Rule of Five in miniature. The compiler now stops you from accidentally copying a texture handle (which would free the same GPU memory twice). Resource bugs become compile errors instead of crashes.

A lightweight entity/component model

A full ECS is overkill for arcade games, but the idea — entities are bags of data, behavior is generic — pays off everywhere. We keep it pragmatic: an Entity holds optional components. Systems are just functions that loop over entities with the data they need.

entity.hpp
#include <optional> #include <vector> struct Transform { Vec2 pos, vel; float rot = 0; }; struct Sprite { int texId; Rectangle src; }; struct Health { int hp = 1; }; struct Entity { bool alive = true; std::optional<Transform> xform; std::optional<Sprite> sprite; std::optional<Health> health; }; // A "system" = a function over entities that have the parts it needs void MoveSystem(std::vector<Entity>& es, float dt) { for (auto& e : es) if (e.alive && e.xform) e.xform->pos = e.xform->pos + e.xform->vel * dt; }
Data-oriented thinking.
Instead of deep inheritance trees (Enemy : Actor : GameObject), an entity simply has components. Want a flying turret? Give it Transform + Sprite + Health but no Velocity input. Composition beats inheritance for games.

The scene stack

Games are made of states: title, gameplay, pause, game-over. A stack models this perfectly — push Pause on top of Gameplay, then pop it to resume exactly where you left off. Each scene is an interface with update, draw, and lifecycle hooks.

scene.hpp
struct Scene { virtual ~Scene() = default; virtual void update(float dt) = 0; virtual void draw() = 0; virtual bool opaque() const { return true; } // draw under me? }; class SceneStack { std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Scene>> stack_; public: void push(std::unique_ptr<Scene> s) { stack_.push_back(std::move(s)); } void pop() { stack_.pop_back(); } void update(float dt) { if (!stack_.empty()) stack_.back()->update(dt); // only top } void draw() { // find lowest scene we must start drawing from size_t start = stack_.size(); while (start > 0 && !stack_[start-1]->opaque()) start--; if (start > 0) start--; for (size_t i = start; i < stack_.size(); i++) stack_[i]->draw(); // pause overlay over frozen game } };

Using std::unique_ptr means scenes are owned by the stack and destroyed automatically when popped — RAII again, now for whole game states. A transparent pause scene (opaque() == false) lets the frozen gameplay show through underneath.

main.cpp (wiring)
SceneStack scenes; scenes.push(std::make_unique<TitleScene>()); while (!WindowShouldClose()) { scenes.update(GetFrameTime()); BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(BLACK); scenes.draw(); EndDrawing(); }
Gotcha.
Never pop() a scene from inside its own update() — you'd destroy the object whose method is still running. Instead set a flag (e.g. requestPop = true) and have the stack process it after the update returns.
Foundation locked in.
RAII wrappers, an entity/component model, and a scene stack are the spine of this track. The platformer, Metroidvania, RPG, and capstone all build directly on these three files.
🎮 Build This: Engine Core — The Reusable Framework

Genre: engine core / architecture. Concept lesson — it does not start a new game. You refactor one of your earlier arcade games (Space Invaders is ideal) onto the new framework.

  1. Wrap every Texture2D in the RAII Tex class; delete the manual UnloadTexture calls.
  2. Convert aliens, bullets, and the player into Entity objects with components.
  3. Write MoveSystem and a DrawSystem that iterate the entity vector.
  4. Split the game into TitleScene, GameScene, and a transparent PauseScene.
  5. Push PauseScene on P (game freezes, shows through); pop to resume via a deferred flag.
  6. Save this framework as your new template — every later game starts here.
